---
title: "Week 1: Introduction to Earth Observation"
editor: visual
---

\## Summary\
\
In Week 1, I focused on understanding the fundamentals of Earth Observation (EO) and remote sensing, which form the backbone of many modern spatial analysis techniques. The lecture provided an in-depth overview of electromagnetic radiation principles and explained how different sensors capture data. Passive sensors, such as optical cameras on Sentinel or Landsat satellites, detect reflected sunlight, while active sensors like Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) emit their own signals and can acquire data regardless of cloud coverage or lighting conditions. Multi-spectral imaging and spectral signatures were introduced, emphasizing the importance of specific wavelength bands in detecting vegetation, water, and urban surfaces. The concept of the red edge band in Sentinel-2 imagery was highlighted as particularly useful for vegetation monitoring. During practical exercises, I explored Google Earth Engine (GEE) to access Sentinel-2 imagery for London and calculated NDVI maps to visualize the spatial distribution of green areas across different neighborhoods. I also learned about key limitations in EO data, such as cloud coverage, spatial resolution, and temporal revisit frequency, and strategies to mitigate these, including image composites, cloud filtering, and using multiple images over time to improve data reliability.\
\
\## Applications\
\
EO data are widely applied in urban planning and environmental research. For example, NDVI and other vegetation indices are commonly used to monitor urban green space distribution, evaluate heat island effects, and analyze accessibility to green infrastructure. The Dynamic World dataset provides near real-time land cover information based on Sentinel imagery, allowing urban planners to identify areas with limited greenery and prioritize interventions. Integrating EO data with GIS enables complex spatial analyses, such as correlating vegetation coverage with socio-economic indicators, transportation networks, and commercial activity patterns. In my ongoing London retail research, EO-derived NDVI maps can be combined with building density, street network accessibility, and land-use data to explore how environmental factors influence retail distribution, neighborhood vitality, and economic disparities. Beyond academic research, these methods have practical applications in urban climate mitigation, sustainable planning, and environmental monitoring, supporting data-driven decision-making at multiple scales. This practical understanding of EO provides a foundation for linking environmental observations with urban spatial processes and allows more robust interpretation of how the built environment interacts with social and economic patterns.\
\
\## Reflection\
\
Week 1 provided a strong theoretical and practical foundation in EO and GEE workflow. I gained a clear understanding of satellite sensor principles, data acquisition processes, and preprocessing methods. Practically, generating NDVI maps in GEE enhanced my ability to visualize spatial heterogeneity across London neighborhoods and highlighted the importance of accounting for data limitations such as clouds or resolution differences. I can now see the potential to apply these techniques directly to my London retail project by overlaying vegetation indices, street accessibility, and building density with retail density data. Additionally, learning to work with GEE introduces opportunities to scale analyses to larger regions and longer time periods, enabling temporal studies of urban environmental change. Overall, this week strengthened my confidence in using EO data for urban spatial analysis and prepared me for integrating more advanced analytical methods, including machine learning and spatial econometrics, in future practical exercises.
